course_info.title: Ethical Hacking and Penetration Testing
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Penetration Testing Methodology and Legal Framework
learning_objectives: Describe the phases of a professional penetration test
Understand the legal and ethical boundaries of security testing
Draft a Rules of Engagement document
readings: The Web Application Hacker's Handbook Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Ethical hacking is the practice of testing systems with authorization to find vulnerabilities before malicious actors do. The line between ethical and criminal is defined by authorization.
key_concepts: Penetration testing phases: Reconnaissance, Scanning, Exploitation, Post-Exploitation, Reporting
Rules of Engagement and scope definition
Legal frameworks: CFAA, GDPR implications, state laws
Types of tests: black box, white box, gray box
Bug bounty programs and responsible disclosure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walking through a sample Rules of Engagement document and penetration test report

review.value: Legal document defining scope, authorized targets, testing window, emergency contacts, and out-of-scope systems. Must be signed before any testing begins.
Pre-engagement, Intelligence Gathering, Threat Modeling, Vulnerability Analysis, Exploitation, Post-Exploitation, Reporting

learning_objectives: Perform passive and active reconnaissance techniques
Use OSINT tools to gather intelligence on targets
Enumerate subdomains, email addresses, and organizational structure

lecture_notes.introduction: Reconnaissance is the most critical phase of a penetration test. The quality of your intelligence directly determines the success of your exploitation attempts.
key_concepts: Passive reconnaissance: WHOIS, DNS records, certificate transparency logs
Active reconnaissance: port scanning, service enumeration
OSINT tools: Maltego, theHarvester, Shodan, Censys
Google dorking for sensitive information discovery
Social media OSINT and metadata analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Performing full reconnaissance against a lab target using theHarvester, Shodan, and Nmap
setup_requirements: Shodan API key for educational use

review.value: Passive recon gathers information without touching the target (WHOIS, public records). Active recon directly interacts with the target (port scanning), which may be detected.
Using advanced search operators (site:, filetype:, intitle:, inurl:) to discover sensitive files, login portals, and misconfigurations indexed by search engines

learning_objectives: Perform comprehensive port scanning with Nmap
Use NSE scripts for vulnerability detection
readings: SANS GPEN course materials on scanning

lecture_notes.introduction: Scanning and enumeration bridge the gap between reconnaissance and exploitation by identifying exactly what services are running and what vulnerabilities they expose.
key_concepts: Nmap scan types: SYN, TCP connect, UDP, ACK, FIN
Service version detection (-sV) and OS fingerprinting (-O)
Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE) for vulnerability detection
SMB, SNMP, LDAP, and NFS enumeration
Scan evasion techniques: timing, fragmentation, decoys
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full Nmap scan against a Metasploitable target, demonstrating different scan types and NSE scripts

review.value: Half-open scan (-sS) sends SYN, receives SYN-ACK for open ports. Does not complete the handshake, making it faster and less logged than full TCP connect scans.
Nmap Scripting Engine provides categorized scripts (auth, brute, vuln, exploit) that automate service enumeration and vulnerability detection

learning_objectives: Select and configure exploits, payloads, and encoders
Exploit common vulnerabilities in a lab environment
readings: Metasploit: The Penetration Tester's Guide Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Metasploit is the industry-standard exploitation framework. Understanding its architecture and capabilities is essential for any penetration tester.
key_concepts: Metasploit architecture: modules, payloads, encoders, post modules
msfconsole workflow: search, use, set, exploit
Payload types: singles, stagers, stages, Meterpreter
Exploit selection based on enumeration results
Pivoting through compromised hosts
lecture_notes.demonstration: Exploiting EternalBlue (MS17-010) on a lab Windows target, establishing a Meterpreter session, and demonstrating post-exploitation capabilities

review.value: Advanced payload providing an interactive shell with capabilities for file operations, process manipulation, network pivoting, and privilege escalation
Choose exploits based on target OS, service version, and architecture. Match payload to network conditions (reverse vs bind, staged vs stageless).

learning_objectives: Identify and exploit OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities
Use Burp Suite for web application testing
Perform SQL injection and XSS attacks in a lab
readings: The Web Application Hacker's Handbook Ch. 6-10

lecture_notes.introduction: Web applications are the largest attack surface for most organizations. Mastering web application testing is a core penetration testing skill.
key_concepts: OWASP Top 10 2021: Broken Access Control, Injection, Cryptographic Failures
SQL injection: UNION-based, blind, time-based, out-of-band
Cross-Site Scripting: reflected, stored, DOM-based
Burp Suite workflow: proxy, scanner, repeater, intruder
Authentication and session management testing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Exploiting SQL injection and XSS on DVWA and OWASP Juice Shop, demonstrating Burp Suite interception and request manipulation

review.value: Inserting SQL commands into application inputs that are concatenated into database queries. Parameterized queries (prepared statements) are the primary defense.
Injecting malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users. Reflected XSS is in the URL, Stored XSS persists in the database, DOM XSS manipulates the page's JavaScript.

learning_objectives: Perform privilege escalation on Linux and Windows
Maintain persistence and pivot through networks
Write a professional penetration test report

lecture_notes.introduction: Exploitation is only half the job. Post-exploitation demonstrates the real business impact, and the report is what the client pays for.
key_concepts: Linux privilege escalation: SUID binaries, kernel exploits, cron jobs, sudo misconfigurations
Windows privilege escalation: service misconfigurations, unquoted paths, token impersonation
Persistence mechanisms: scheduled tasks, registry run keys, SSH keys
Lateral movement: Pass-the-Hash, PsExec, WMI
Report structure: executive summary, methodology, findings, remediation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Escalating from a low-privilege shell to root/SYSTEM on lab targets using multiple techniques, then drafting findings for the report

review.value: After initial access, the tester attempts to gain higher privileges. On Linux: check SUID, sudo -l, writable cron jobs, kernel version. On Windows: check services, scheduled tasks, token privileges.
Executive summary for leadership, technical details for IT staff. Each finding: title, severity, evidence, business impact, remediation steps. The report is the deliverable.

questions.question: You are conducting a penetration test against a company's external perimeter. Your Nmap SYN scan of the web server shows port 443 open with an expired SSL certificate and port 8443 running Apache Tomcat 9.0.30. Describe your next three steps with specific tools and techniques.
questions.answer: Step 1: Enumerate the SSL/TLS configuration on port 443 using sslscan or testssl.sh to identify weak ciphers, protocol versions, and certificate issues beyond expiration. Step 2: Run Nmap NSE scripts (http-enum, http-title, http-methods) against both ports, and use Nikto for web server misconfiguration scanning on both 443 and 8443. Step 3: For Tomcat 9.0.30 specifically, check for CVE-2020-1938 (Ghostcat/AJP vulnerability), attempt to access /manager and /host-manager with default credentials (tomcat:tomcat), and use Burp Suite to spider the application for additional attack surface.

rubric.key: Web server enumeration with appropriate tools

questions.question: Explain the difference between a SYN scan (-sS) and a TCP connect scan (-sT) in Nmap. Under what circumstances would you be forced to use -sT instead of -sS, and what are the security implications?
questions.answer: SYN scan sends a SYN packet and waits for SYN-ACK (open) or RST (closed) without completing the handshake. It requires raw socket privileges (root/admin). TCP connect scan completes the full three-way handshake using the OS's connect() system call. You are forced to use -sT when running Nmap as a non-root user, when scanning through certain SOCKS proxies, or when IPv6 scanning on some systems. Security implications: -sT creates a full connection that is logged by the target's TCP stack and application logs, making detection much more likely. -sS is stealthier as the half-open connection is often not logged by applications.

questions.question: Given the following PHP code: $query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " . $_GET['id']; Demonstrate a UNION-based SQL injection payload that would extract all usernames and passwords from a 'users' table with columns 'username' and 'password'. Explain each part of the payload.
questions.answer: Payload: ?id=1 UNION SELECT username, password FROM users-- Explanation: '1' satisfies the original query. UNION appends a second SELECT that extracts username and password columns. The '--' comments out any trailing SQL. The UNION SELECT must have the same number of columns as the original query. If the original has more columns, pad with NULL: ?id=1 UNION SELECT username, password, NULL, NULL FROM users--

rubric.key: Explains each component of the payload

questions.question: You have a Meterpreter session on a Windows 10 workstation as a standard user. Describe two specific techniques you would attempt for privilege escalation, including the Meterpreter commands or modules you would use.
questions.answer: Technique 1: Run 'getsystem' which attempts multiple techniques including Named Pipe Impersonation and Token Duplication. If that fails, use 'run post/multi/recon/local_exploit_suggester' to identify kernel exploits matching the target's patch level. Technique 2: Check for unquoted service paths with 'run post/windows/gather/enum_unquoted_service_paths'. If found, generate a payload with msfvenom matching the service path, upload with 'upload', and restart the vulnerable service. The service will execute our payload with SYSTEM privileges due to the unquoted path parsing.

rubric.key: Explains why each technique grants elevated privileges

major_assignments.title: Full-Scope Penetration Test
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive penetration test against the provided lab network containing multiple hosts with various operating systems and services. Produce a professional penetration test report.
objectives: Execute all phases of a penetration test methodically
Identify and exploit vulnerabilities across multiple attack vectors
Produce a professional-quality penetration test report
deliverables: Penetration test report with executive summary and technical details
Evidence documentation: screenshots, command outputs, exploit code
Remediation recommendations prioritized by CVSS severity
Methodology section describing tools and techniques used

criteria: Both network and web application vectors tested
Professional report format following PTES template
Clear executive summary for non-technical audience
Each finding includes evidence, impact, and remediation
Risk ratings justified with CVSS scores
Comprehensive penetration test covering all accessible attack vectors
Multiple successful exploits with documented evidence chains
Professional report suitable for delivery to a real client
Actionable remediation plan with prioritized findings

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of penetration testing concepts and methodology knowledge

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on labs covering reconnaissance, exploitation, and post-exploitation

major_assignments.description: Full-scope penetration test with professional report

midterm_examination.description: Practical exam: timed penetration test of a lab environment

final_project.description: Capstone penetration test against a complex multi-host environment

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in course failure.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be discussed but all exploitation and reporting must be individual work. Sharing exploits or flags is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All testing must be conducted within the authorized lab environment. Testing external systems without written authorization is a criminal offense and grounds for immediate expulsion.
course_policies.lab_safety: Students must sign the lab ethics agreement before accessing any tools. Unauthorized use of penetration testing tools outside the lab will result in course failure and disciplinary action.
